name: humanity (his creation
shortDescription: In Sumerian mythology, humanity was created by the god Enki (also known as Ea) and the mother goddess Nammu to serve the gods and relieve them of labor. The creation story highlights the relationship between gods and humans in their belief system.
description: In Sumerian mythology, the creation of humanity is attributed primarily to Enki (Ea in Akkadian), the god of wisdom, fresh water, intelligence, and mischief, and to the primordial mother goddess Nammu. The gods, weary of their own labor, desired beings to perform the tasks necessary for the maintenance of the cosmos. Nammu, the goddess of the primeval sea, suggested to her son Enki that he create such beings. Enki, with the aid of other deities, fashioned humanity from clay, imbued with divine essence. The specific details of the creation vary across different texts, but the general narrative involves Enki’s ingenuity in shaping the clay and Nammu’s role in giving birth to the first humans. These humans were intended to serve the gods, providing them with food, shelter, and worship. The myth emphasizes the hierarchical structure of Sumerian society, with the gods at the top and humans in a subservient role. However, the relationship was not purely one-sided; humans also received protection and guidance from the gods. The creation myth reflects the Sumerian understanding of their place in the universe and their relationship with the divine. The tales of early humans and their interactions with the gods are found in various cuneiform texts, including the Atrahasis epic and fragments of other creation myths. The creation of humanity is a central theme in Mesopotamian religion and provides insight into their worldview, ethics, and understanding of the divine-human relationship.

sources.title: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature
sources.author: University of Oxford

sources.title: Before Philosophy
sources.author: Henri Frankfort, H.A. Frankfort, John A. Wilson, Thorkild Jacobsen
